title: Common Complexities

project_usage: Choosing the right algorithm for a given constraint: n <= 10^6 rules out O(n^2), demands O(n log n) or better
Capacity planning: knowing that a hash table lookup is O(1) vs. a sorted array binary search at O(log n) informs architectural decisions

overview.what: The common complexity classes form a hierarchy of growth rates that virtually all algorithms fall into: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(n^3), O(2^n), and O(n!). Each class represents a qualitatively different scaling regime — moving one step up the hierarchy can mean the difference between an algorithm finishing in milliseconds or not finishing in your lifetime.
overview.why: Knowing the hierarchy lets you immediately judge whether an algorithm is feasible for a given input size. When an interviewer says n can be up to 10^5, you instantly know O(n^2) is borderline and O(n^3) is impossible, and you need an O(n log n) or O(n) approach.
where_used: Constraint-based algorithm selection in competitive programming and interviews
Load testing and capacity planning: will this O(n log n) sort handle 10 million records within our SLA?

why_learning_this: It provides the mental ruler that makes every future complexity analysis instant — you stop calculating and start recognizing.
The constraint-to-complexity mapping (n <= 10^6 means you need O(n log n) or better) is used in virtually every timed coding assessment.
It reveals why certain classes of problems are fundamentally intractable: O(2^n) on n = 50 is more operations than atoms in the universe.

common_mistakes.title: Assuming O(n log n) is 'basically O(n)'
common_mistakes.explanation: While log n grows slowly, it is NOT a constant. At n = 10^9, log2(n) is about 30 — so an O(n log n) algorithm does 30 billion operations versus 1 billion for O(n). That 30x factor can push you past the time limit.

common_mistakes.title: Forgetting that O(n^2) explodes faster than intuition suggests
common_mistakes.explanation: At n = 1000, O(n^2) means a million operations — fine. At n = 100,000, it means 10 billion operations — likely too slow. The jump from 'works on test cases' to 'times out on the judge' is exactly this transition.

common_mistakes.title: Not recognizing O(1) amortized vs. O(1) worst-case
common_mistakes.explanation: A dynamic array append is O(1) amortized but O(n) worst-case (when it resizes). Confusing these can lead to incorrect analysis of tight inner loops or real-time systems.

recall_questions.q: List the common complexity classes from fastest to slowest growth.
recall_questions.answer: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2), O(n^3), O(2^n), O(n!).

recall_questions.q: Given a problem constraint of n <= 10^6, what is the maximum acceptable time complexity and why?
recall_questions.answer: O(n log n), which gives roughly 2 * 10^7 operations — well within the typical 10^8 operations-per-second budget. O(n^2) would be 10^12 operations, which is far too slow.

recall_questions.q: Name one algorithm that runs in each of these classes: O(1), O(log n), O(n), O(n log n), O(n^2).
recall_questions.answer: O(1): hash table lookup. O(log n): binary search. O(n): linear scan for max. O(n log n): merge sort. O(n^2): bubble sort.

understanding_checks.question: You need to sort 10 million integers. You have two options: an O(n^2) insertion sort and an O(n log n) merge sort. At roughly 10^8 simple operations per second, estimate the wall-clock time for each and decide.
understanding_checks.answer: Insertion sort: (10^7)^2 = 10^14 operations, roughly 10^6 seconds (about 11 days). Merge sort: 10^7 * 23 = 2.3 * 10^8 operations, roughly 2.3 seconds. Merge sort is the only feasible option.
understanding_checks.why: This drives home that the gap between O(n^2) and O(n log n) is not a minor optimization — it is the difference between seconds and days. The complexity class is the single most important factor at scale.

understanding_checks.question: An algorithm correctly solves a problem for n = 100 in under a second, but times out when n = 10,000. Without seeing the code, what complexity class do you suspect and why?
understanding_checks.answer: Most likely O(n^2) or O(n^3). At n = 100, n^2 = 10,000 operations (fast). At n = 10,000, n^2 = 10^8 (borderline) and n^3 = 10^12 (certainly times out). The 100x increase in input causing a timeout strongly suggests a polynomial of degree 2 or higher.
understanding_checks.why: This is the practical skill of reverse-engineering complexity from observed behavior — a direct application of knowing the growth rates of each class.

aha_moment.code:
# How long does each take if one operation = 1 nanosecond?
# n = 100
# O(n)       = 100 ns         (instant)
# O(n log n) = 664 ns         (instant)
# O(n^2)     = 10,000 ns      (instant)
# O(2^n)     = 1.27 * 10^21 ns = 40,000+ YEARS

# n is just 100. Not a million. One hundred.
aha_moment.prediction: O(2^n) on n = 100 will take maybe a few minutes at most.
aha_moment.common_guess: All complexities are manageable when n is only 100.
aha_moment.why: O(2^100) is approximately 1.27 * 10^30 operations. At a billion operations per second, that is 4 * 10^13 years — thousands of times longer than the age of the universe. Exponential growth does not just slow down your program; it makes it physically impossible to complete. This is why NP-hard problems are fundamentally different from polynomial ones.

practice_tasks.title: Map constraints to the tightest feasible complexity
practice_tasks.prompt: For each constraint below, determine the maximum complexity class that will run within approximately 10^8 operations and suggest an algorithm pattern that achieves it: (a) n <= 10, (b) n <= 1000, (c) n <= 10^5, (d) n <= 10^7.
practice_tasks.starter_code:
# Fill in the complexity and a matching pattern:
# (a) n <= 10     -> O(?) -- pattern: ?
# (b) n <= 1000   -> O(?) -- pattern: ?
# (c) n <= 10^5   -> O(?) -- pattern: ?
# (d) n <= 10^7   -> O(?) -- pattern: ?
practice_tasks.solution: (a) O(n!) or O(2^n) — brute force / backtracking is feasible. (b) O(n^2) — nested loops, simple DP. (c) O(n log n) — sorting-based or divide-and-conquer. (d) O(n) — single pass, hash map, prefix sums.
